description: Per-model inference latency on the Radxa Cubie A7A (Allwinner A733). NPU = VeriSilicon VIP9000 NANO-DI, run with the ai-sdk vpm_run tool (reported 'profile inference time', averaged over 100 loops; a dummy zero input is used - latency is input-independent for a fixed graph). CPU = raw per-model TFLite/XNNPACK fp32 inference (tflite-runtime 2.13, median of 100 loops, pinned to the Cortex-A76 with taskset) - the SAME scope as the NPU numbers: pure inference, no pre/post.
board: Radxa Cubie A7A, A733, kernel 5.15.147-21-a733, /dev/vipcore, ai-sdk viplite v2.0 (2.0.3.2-AW-2024-08-30)

npu_io_overhead.note: vpm_run 'run time' (wall: input DMA write + dispatch + output read) minus 'profile inference time' (pure compute), face_landmarks_detector int16, 20 loops, no layer profiling

fp16_root_cause.note: cycle counters from vpm_run: same effective clock, ~29x more cycles executed in fp16 -> the fp16 graph does not map to the NANO-DI's integer MAC arrays and falls back to a much slower programmable path. Hardware mapping limit, not an I/O or config issue.

findings: Fair iso-scope comparison (pure inference vs pure inference): NPU int16 4.31 ms vs CPU fp32 24.24 ms (1x A76) = 5.6x faster; vs 16.95 ms (2x A76) = 3.9x faster. Both are near-lossless (int16 0.12 px vs fp32-reference 0 px), so this is also iso-accuracy in practice.
Against the full MediaPipe CPU frame (~35 ms end-to-end incl. pre/post) the NPU inference is ~8x faster - a different scope, stated separately on purpose.

fp16 root cause found via cycle counters: ~29x more cycles at the same clock. The NANO-DI's fast MAC arrays are integer-only; fp16 convolutions fall back to a slow programmable path. Unfixable by configuration - int16 is the deploy precision on this NPU.

NPU I/O overhead is small: ~0.25 ms per inference for the largest model (256x256x3 int16 input, DMA write + output read + dispatch), ~7% of compute. Network create/prepare (~6 ms) is one-time at startup.
int16 is near-lossless (0.12 px mean landmark error vs reference - see fidelity.json), so no accuracy is traded for the speed.
The CPU has no int16 execution path for these models (XNNPACK computes in fp32 from fp16 weights), so fp32-CPU vs int16-NPU at matched output accuracy is the fair comparison.
